tips: Most shops and restaurants close on Sundays and some on Monday mornings.
Tipping is not expected in France as service is included (service compris), but rounding up is appreciated.
Pharmacies (green cross sign) are excellent for health advice, not just medication.
Always greet shopkeepers with 'Bonjour' when entering -- it's considered rude not to.
Tap water is safe to drink everywhere. Ask for 'une carafe d'eau' for free water at restaurants.
Lunch is the main meal, typically from 2-4 PM. Restaurants may not open for dinner until 9 PM.
Siesta culture means many shops close from 2-5 PM, especially outside big cities.
Tapas are often free with drinks in cities like Granada and Salamanca.
Be alert for pickpockets on La Rambla in Barcelona and in crowded metro stations.
Learn to say 'por favor' (please) and 'gracias' (thank you) -- it goes a long way.
Many churches require covered shoulders and knees for entry -- carry a scarf or wrap.
Espresso is drunk standing at the bar. Sitting at a table costs more (coperto).
Validate train tickets before boarding at the small machines on platforms.
Italian drivers can be aggressive -- be extra cautious when crossing streets.
Water from public fountains (nasoni) in Rome is safe and refreshing.
Many shops and restaurants are cash-only (Bargeld), especially smaller ones.
Shops are closed on Sundays by law (except at train stations and airports).
Tap water is safe but not always free in restaurants -- ask for Leitungswasser.
Recycling is taken seriously. Return bottles with Pfand deposit for a refund.
Jaywalking is frowned upon and occasionally fined, even if no cars are coming.
The weather is unpredictable year-round -- always carry a waterproof layer.
Driving is on the left side of the road. Look right first when crossing streets.
Tipping 10-15% is customary in sit-down restaurants but not in pubs.
The Tube (London Underground) gets very hot in summer -- carry water.
Many museums and galleries in London are free to enter.
Tipping 15-20% is expected at restaurants, bars, and for taxi rides.
Sales tax is not included in displayed prices -- expect to pay more at checkout.
Distances between cities are vast. Consider flying or renting a car for long trips.
Free refills on soft drinks and coffee are common at most restaurants.
Check if you need an ESTA visa waiver before traveling.
Carry cash -- many places, especially outside Tokyo, don't accept cards.
Tipping is not customary and can even be considered rude.
Remove shoes when entering homes, temples, and some restaurants (look for shoe racks).
Trains run exactly on time. Being even a minute late means missing your train.
It's considered rude to eat or drink while walking. Stop and consume food at the stall.
japan.plugType: Type A (two flat pins, no ground)

tips: Always show respect to images and statues of the King and Buddha.
Remove shoes before entering temples and homes.
Bargaining is expected at markets but not in malls or convenience stores.
Street food is safe and excellent -- eat at busy stalls with high turnover.
The rainy season (June-October) brings heavy but short afternoon downpours.
thailand.plugType: Type A/B/C (varies -- bring a universal adapter)

tips: Slip, slop, slap: slip on a shirt, slop on sunscreen, slap on a hat. UV is extreme.
Don't swim at unpatrolled beaches. Always swim between the red and yellow flags.
Australia has strict biosecurity. Declare all food, plants, and animal products on arrival.
Distances are huge. Plan drives carefully and carry extra water for outback travel.
Tipping is not expected but rounding up is appreciated.
Drink bottled water only. Avoid ice from unknown sources.
Negotiate taxi fares before getting in, or use ride-share apps.
Street food is amazing but stick to busy stalls where food is cooked fresh.
Keep copies of your passport separate from the original.
Learn some basic Spanish -- it's warmly received and helpful outside tourist areas.
Island ferries can be delayed or cancelled due to strong winds (meltemi).
Carry cash for tavernas and small shops, especially on islands.
Tap water is safe on the mainland but not always on smaller islands.
Topless sunbathing is common on many beaches, but nudity only on designated ones.
Try to visit popular sites like the Acropolis early morning to avoid crowds and heat.
Lisbon is very hilly -- wear comfortable walking shoes with good grip.
Try pastéis de nata (custard tarts) at a traditional pastelaria.
Portugal is very safe, but be cautious of petty theft in tourist areas.
Tap water is safe to drink throughout the country.
Many restaurants offer a 'prato do dia' (dish of the day) which is usually excellent value.
Bargaining is expected in bazaars and markets. Start at about half the asking price.
Remove shoes when entering mosques. Women should cover their heads.
Turkish breakfast is legendary -- try a full spread at a local café.
The Istanbul Kart is essential for public transport in Istanbul (metro, tram, ferry).
Tap water is generally safe but locals prefer bottled water.
Dress modestly, especially when visiting medinas and rural areas.
Bargaining is expected in souks. Never accept the first price.
Riads (traditional guesthouses) offer a much more authentic experience than hotels.
Drink mint tea generously offered by locals -- refusing can be considered rude.
Use registered guides in cities to avoid scams and get the best experience.
Drink only bottled water. Check the seal is intact before buying.
Dress conservatively when visiting temples and religious sites.
Negotiate auto-rickshaw and taxi fares before getting in.
Street food is incredible but start slowly to let your stomach adjust.
The left hand is considered unclean -- use your right hand for eating and greetings.
india.plugType: Type C/D/M (varies -- bring a universal adapter)

tips: T-money card works on all public transport in Seoul and major cities.
Convenience stores (7-Eleven, CU, GS25) are excellent for cheap, quality meals.
Wi-Fi is available almost everywhere, including public buses and subways.
Take off shoes when entering homes and some traditional restaurants.
Tipping is not expected and can cause confusion.
Avoid showing expensive jewelry, phones, or cameras in public.
Learn basic Portuguese phrases -- English is not widely spoken outside tourist areas.
Use ride-share apps instead of hailing taxis on the street.
Tap water is treated but locals prefer filtered water. Buy bottled to be safe.
Always carry a copy of your passport, not the original.
Tipping 15-20% is customary in restaurants and for services.
Weather varies drastically by region. Check conditions for your specific destination.
Many areas are remote -- download offline maps and carry emergency supplies.
Canadian dollars look different from US dollars. Check your change.
Be bear-aware when hiking. Carry bear spray in national parks.
Dress modestly, especially at religious sites. Women should carry a headscarf.
Negotiate prices before hiring guides, camels, or buying at markets.
Drink bottled water only. Avoid raw vegetables washed in tap water.
Carry small bills for tips (baksheesh), which is customary for most services.
Visit major sites early in the morning to avoid intense afternoon heat.
Cyclists have right of way in most situations. Watch out for bike lanes when walking.
An OV-chipkaart is the easiest way to use all public transport.
Most places accept cards, including contactless payments.
Coffeeshops sell cannabis; coffee houses (koffiehuizen) sell actual coffee.
Wind can be fierce, especially near the coast. Bring a windproof jacket.
Dubrovnik and Split get extremely crowded in summer -- visit in shoulder season if possible.
Croatian kuna has been replaced by the Euro (since Jan 2023).
Water from public fountains is safe to drink in most Croatian cities.
Island hopping by ferry is easy but book ahead in summer.
Many beaches are pebbly -- bring water shoes for comfort.
Crossing the street can be overwhelming -- walk slowly and steadily, and traffic flows around you.
Street food is some of the best in the world. Follow the crowds to find the best stalls.
Bargaining is expected at markets. Be polite and smile.
Carry small bills -- many places cannot break large notes.
Motorbike taxis (xe om) and Grab are cheap ways to get around.
vietnam.plugType: Type A/C (varies -- bring a universal adapter)

tips: Weather changes rapidly -- dress in waterproof, windproof layers.
Never walk on glacier ice without a certified guide and proper gear.
The Ring Road (Route 1) is the best way to see the country -- allow at least 7-10 days.
Hot springs and geothermal pools are everywhere -- always bring a swimsuit.
Iceland is very expensive. Self-catering and camping can save significant money.
Respect Maori culture -- learn basic greetings like 'Kia ora' (hello).
Biosecurity is strict. Declare all food, plant material, and outdoor gear on arrival.
Distances look short on maps but roads are winding. Allow extra driving time.
The UV index is extreme, even on cloudy days. Wear SPF 50+ sunscreen.
Free campsites exist but check local regulations before camping.
new zealand.plugType: Type I (angled flat pins, same as Australia)

tips: Switzerland is extremely expensive. Budget carefully for meals and transport.
The Swiss Travel Pass offers excellent value for unlimited train, bus, and boat travel.
Tap water is safe and high quality everywhere, including public fountains.
Quiet hours are strictly observed -- avoid noise after 10 PM and on Sundays.
Carry CHF cash as not all mountain huts and small shops accept cards.
switzerland.plugType: Type J (three-pin round, unique to Switzerland)

europe: Most of Europe uses the Euro, but some countries have their own currency. Check before you go.
European plug types vary but Type C (two round pins) works in most countries.
Trains are an excellent way to travel between European cities. Book in advance for better prices.
Many European cities have excellent free walking tours -- just tip your guide.
Tap water is generally safe across Western Europe.
asia: Respect local customs around temples and religious sites -- remove shoes and dress modestly.
Street food is often safer than restaurant food because you can see it being prepared.
Bargaining is expected in markets across most of Asia.
Download offline maps and translation apps before you go.
Check visa requirements well in advance -- some countries need pre-arranged visas.
americas: Distances in the Americas are vast -- domestic flights can save days of travel.
Tap water quality varies significantly by country. When in doubt, drink bottled.
Tipping culture varies widely across the Americas. Research your specific destination.
Spanish is the most widely spoken language in Latin America, but Portuguese is spoken in Brazil.
Altitude sickness can be an issue in Andean countries. Acclimatize slowly.
africa: Yellow fever vaccination certificates may be required at borders.
Take anti-malaria precautions in tropical and sub-tropical regions.
Carry US dollars as they're widely accepted as a secondary currency.
Internet connectivity can be unreliable outside major cities. Download offline resources.
Respect local customs and ask before photographing people.
oceania: UV radiation is extreme in Australia and New Zealand. Wear high-SPF sunscreen daily.
Biosecurity is taken very seriously. Declare all food and biological materials.
Distances are vast -- plan your itinerary carefully.
Water sports and outdoor activities are a must. Pack accordingly.
Respect Indigenous cultures and sacred sites.
middle_east: Dress conservatively, especially in religious areas. Women should carry a headscarf.
Alcohol is restricted or prohibited in several countries. Check local laws.
Fridays are the holy day -- many businesses close or have reduced hours.
Haggling is expected in souks and traditional markets.
Carry cash in local currency for smaller purchases and markets.
